utterances: Yeah, one of several, yeah.
Just very disappointed.
Thanks for hearing my comment.
David, thanks for the call.
And Richard, that brings us to one final question.
If this doesn't work, if the relief wells don't work, what's the worst-case scenario here?
So what you have to do is find some other ways to nudge people into hand washing.

It's a deeply habitual habit, something that you probably learned in childhood and you've always done the same thing - either/or with washing with soap or not washing with soap or with other - as most people unfortunately don't - don't do it.
Well, everyone keeps saying, well, the relief will have to work eventually.
And if they - it's - if they don't, this - the oil will just keep spilling and spilling and spilling.
So they have to - there has to be something if a relief well simply doesn't work.
And - but we have - but I've been asking around.

I have not found a single example so far - although I'd be interested in knowing if anyone knows one - of a relief well like this, which is called a bottom kill, that did not work out, that actually failed.
And that would be very scary indeed if there were such an occasion.
But I've been looking around, I have not yet heard of one.
But that's not to say they don't...
If you have information about that, send us an e-mail: talk@npr.org.
We'll pass it along to Richard.
I appreciate that.
Richard Harris, thanks very much for your time today.
Appreciate it.
Good to be with you, Neal.
Starting this week, Netflix revisits an attack on Paris.
In November 2015, gunmen attacked a theater and other locations.
A new documentary about the attacks is called ""Fluctuat Nec Mergitur."" There's a story behind that French title that reflects the larger story of Paris.
Here's reporter Jake Cigainero.
(SOUNDBITE OF JACQUES DUTRONC SONG, ""IL EST CINQ HEURES, PARIS S'EVEILLE"")
The riveting three-part documentary opens with the sun coming up over Paris on November 13, 2015 - just another day, the documentary seems to suggest.
But it wasn't.
That evening, nine terrorists attacked the stadium outside Paris, six cafes and the Bataclan music venue.
Survivors, alongside firemen, police and former President Francois Hollande give chilling, minute-by-minute accounts of the chaos as they lived it.
French filmmaker Jules Naudet said the documentary borrows part of its title from Paris' Latin motto, fluctuat nec mergitur, which means...
Moved by the sea but never sunk, which represented, I think, the people and that night.
Naudet and his brother, co-director Gedeon Naudet, understand watching three hours of terrorism can be a turnoff.
But their message, Jules says, is hope and resilience.
We wanted to convey that the moment where you see the worst of humanity, the best is always present and is always stronger.
We need that.
There is a hunger for that humanity and that beauty, even at the worst places.
(SOUNDBITE OF MUSIC)
That night, gunmen burst into a rock concert at the Bataclan and went on a shooting rampage.
They also took 11 hostages in an hours-long standoff with police.
The thrust of the documentary follows the harrowing experience of these hostages.
This part of the attacks has never been told so comprehensively.
Six of the 11 hostages share their stories, from the moment they say goodbye to their families as they leave for a concert to the final police assault and, finally, their lives today.
They recount being forced to help the attackers and how they formed bonds with total strangers to survive.
Here, a woman - only identified as Marie in the documentary - also talks about surreal moments of unexpected humor, such as the terrorist using her phone to talk to police.
(SOUNDBITE OF DOCUMENTARY, ""NOVEMBER 13: ATTACK ON PARIS"")

(Through interpreter) My phone case is a smiling green whale, so seeing this guy who has killed I don't know how many people holding that was funny - but not, because on the screen is a photo of my daughters.

The Naudets say this is their most personal project since their Emmy-winning documentary ""9/11."" They were making a film about a Manhattan fire brigade at the time of the attacks on the World Trade Center.
As survivors, Jules says the ""November 13"" documentary is a continuation of their own therapy, and he hopes it will help others who have lived through attacks around the world.
Terrorism has no borders anymore, and so I think it will speak to people who survive in Texas, in New York and all that, or in London or in Madrid or in Barcelona.
I think it talks to all of us.
For NPR News, I'm Jake Cigainero in Paris.
(SOUNDBITE OF POPPY ACKROYD SONG, ""RAIN"")
It's Tuesday, and time to read from your emails and Web comments.
Laura Sullivans reports on the bail system pulled back the curtain on an industry that often leaves poor people stranded in jail while they wait for trial.
John Nisbet is an assistant public defender in Tennessee.
He emailed to tell us: I find the bail bond system to be brutal.
This week in court, I will have 25 to 30 percent of my clients who will plead guilty merely in order to get out of jail.
When you get arrested, youre taken to jail and cannot access any of your possessions in order to make bail.
So if you don't have a relative or friend who's on the outside who can contact a bail company and then make the bail on the promise of being repaid, youre out of luck.
Maybe instead of being so concerned for people in jail, we should be more concerned for the people they have hurt and for protection of society from them.
Yes, some bail procedures should be tweaked, but the overwhelming majority of people in jail put themselves there by their actions.
That by email from Cliff Ratner(ph) in Wichita.
And - well, there's no delicate transition here.
Cheech and Chong lit up his joint last week.
Many of you are huge fans and show no signs of memory problems.
I remember like it was yesterday sitting in front of my cousin's stereo, me and a couple of cousins listening to ""Big Bambu"" over and over, wrote Richard, a listener in Alabama.
I'm now a 46-year-old attorney.
Just a few months ago, I finally bought the DVD of ""Being There,"" and ""Basketball Jones"" still cracks me up.
I and friends still threw out Cheech and Chong lines.
Sign ze papers, old man, is one all attorneys know.
Cheech and Chong added a bit of activism to their national tour this time, putting their celebrity behind initiatives to legalize marijuana, which did not amuse Mary Cranston(ph) in Virginia.
She wrote: Cheech and Chong are inspired comedians.
I've enjoyed their humor since my college days, but marijuana is illegal.
To let them plug its use on NPR to which my 17 and 20-year-old sons listen has made my parenting more difficult.
Come on, folks.
Help us out here.
If you have comments, questions or corrections for us, the best way to reach us is by email.
The address is talk@npr.org.
Please let us know where youre writing from and give us some help on how to pronounce your name.
He can remain - he can say that.
He's a young man and obviously, under a lot of pressure, so...

But the nature of that package - tax cuts versus measures targeted for the middle class - is likely to lead to a battle royale with congressional Democrats that could dominate the burgeoning election campaign.
Mr. Bush acknowledged that many Americans are anxious about the economy.
What he did not say is what many Republican politicians, including candidates, are anxious about a possible recession as November election approaches.
The (unintelligible) mantra resonates today.
This is Daniel Schorr.
Here are some of the concerns elections officials have across the country, fraud, long lines, and too few ballots.
It is especially worse in states where the election is close.
From member station KWMU in St. Louis, Adam Allington reports on the preparations in Missouri.
Bettie Williams is the registration supervisor for St. Louis city.
She's part of a team that inspects, stamps, scans, and enters some of Missouri's 325,000 new registrations into a statewide voter database.
On the closing registration alone, the different organizations draft off 6,000 registrations.
That was just the close of registration.
By most accounts, organizations such as ACORN and Missouri Pro Vote have added as many as 50,000 voters to the rolls.
But as we've seen across the country, steep jumps in registration also breed controversy.
Scott Leiendecker is the Republican director of elections in St. Louis.
Sitting on his desk is a packet of 100 registration cards sent in from the Neighborhood Assistance Corporation of America nearly one week after the October 8th registration deadline.
You know, they dropped these registration cards, and as we were kind of just thumbing through them, you see, you know, the date of 8/23, you know, 9/21, 8/23, 8/23.
I don't know if they had these in their couch cushion or what.
But now, there's a 100 folks that, you know, are assuming that they're going to be able to go to the polls on election day and vote without a problem.
Well, you know, they've got a problem.
And those 100 voters aren't the only ones with a problem.
Missouri Republicans have a fundamental problem with groups like ACORN, which they claim are committing registration fraud to aid Democrats.
But Missouri Secretary of State Robin Carnahan says, no matter what Republicans say, instances of actual in-person voter fraud are virtually nil.
I think we've got the cleanest, best voter list we've ever had in the history of our state.
Carnahan points out that a new federally-mandated registration database, in use since 2006, makes scrubbing the rolls easier than ever.

In 2004, and all those prior elections, it was county by county sort of kept their own voter rolls, and sometimes, you know, if you moved from St. Louis County to St. Charles County, you might be on both of those lists.
That doesn't happen any more because we now have a statewide list that we can get rid of duplicates.
With the predicted 85 percent turn out, Carnahan says her worry is making sure everyone who goes to vote gets to vote.
First, there's the matter of training thousands of poll workers to operate the state's new electronic voting equipment.
Everyone go ahead and look at your screen.
You're seeing - it's in election mode at the top of the screen.
On November 4th, it's going to say, general election city of St. Louis...
Paul Schmidt is training a group of computer science students to serve as tech assistants on Missouri's new touch screen voting machines.
It's been a little over two years that we've been using the machines, and we've had nothing but success.
The only caveat to that is this particular election coming up in November.
We estimate that there will be many many voters that haven't voted in at least the last four years and possibly more.
Another concern is over potential confusion over I.D.
requirements.
Shortly before the '06 congressional elections, the state legislature passed a bill requiring voters to show photo I.D.
at polling places.
That measure was later overturned by the state's Supreme Court, but confusion remained.
Matthew Potter, deputy director of the St. Louis Board of Elections, says, in lieu of photo I.D., a pay stub, a banks statement, or a utility bill will all suffice.
We're doing everything we can to let people know they don't need a photo I.D.
It's on the Internet on our website.
It's going to be on every voter notification card.
During our judge trainings, we have our judges recite after the trainer two times, no photo I.D.
is required.
Finally, once the voter gets into the booth, there is the ballot.
And it's a long ballot, 30 to 40 local choices, 5 statewide measures to consider, plus the congressional and presidential candidates.
And unlike previous years, there's no longer an option of pulling a single lever for a straight party vote.
Precincts are encouraging people to vote during off hours to control lines.
And with Missouri entering the final week of the campaign as a hotly contested swing state, election officials are hoping that it's the candidates, not the process, that make the headlines.
For NPR News, I'm Adam Allington in St. Louis.

Now we're going to get another perspective on President Trump's foreign policy approach from someone who has a different relationship with him but also knows him well - Trump biographer Timothy O'Brien, author of ""TrumpNation: The Art Of Being The Donald."" We wanted to hear Mr. O'Brien's perspective on this week's events since the president has no prior experience in foreign policy and trade policy since before he took the White House, and we thought his approach to other issues might yield some clues.
And Mr. O'Brien started by explaining what he thinks is behind the president's thinking on foreign policy.
